ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'cola soft drink' class; no specific 'less sugar' variant exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cola-type soft drinks broadly; no specific reduced-sugar variant code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for reduced-sugar cola beverage identified in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 320 ml can or bottle of Coca-Cola Original Less Sugar, as sold in Singapore convenience stores and supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo Database — Coca-Cola Original Less Sugar, product code 909D
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for product code 909D, Coca-Cola Original Less Sugar (confidence 0.95 per input record). Saturated fat (0.0 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.0 g/100 g) are consistent with the Coca-Cola product formulation and corroborated by the Singapore Nutrition Information Panel data recorded in the input record. HPB Foodo is an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Cola-type carbonated soft drink, regular (GI = 63, glucose reference).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Coca-Cola Original Less Sugar in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest published peer-reviewed entry is regular cola-type carbonated soft drink (GI = 63, glucose reference) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:12), which is an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2. The less-sugar variant contains 9.94 g sugar/100 g versus approximately 10.6 g/100 g for full-sugar cola; GI 63 is used as a conservative defensible analogous estimate. GL per 320 ml serving = (63 × 10.36 × 320 / 100) / 100 = 20.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.94 g sugar/100 g; SSBs independently raise blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention regardless of the beverage's low sodium content (3 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage; the AHA recommends limiting SSBs to no more than 450 kcal per week, and this product's sugar load independently elevates cardiovascular and blood pressure risk — switch to Coca-Cola Zero Sugar or water.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with 9.94 g free sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes with no fibre-mediated attenuation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This product is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; as a sugar-sweetened beverage it triggers the SSB clinical gate regardless of the 'less sugar' labelling — liquid free sugars raise blood glucose faster than equivalent solid carbohydrates, and the product should be replaced with Coca-Cola Zero Sugar or unsweetened beverages.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular SSB consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased visceral adiposity, and endothelial dysfunction; this product contributes 9.94 g free sugar per 100 g with no protective nutrients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise SSB intake; the free sugar in this beverage contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and weight gain — limit to occasional consumption and prefer zero-sugar alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High free sugar (9.94 g/100 g) with zero fibre and no anti-inflammatory nutrients; sugar at this level promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this SSB; the free sugar load promotes systemic inflammation with no offsetting anti-inflammatory factors — switch to Coca-Cola Zero Sugar and increase intake of omega-3-rich and antioxidant-rich foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coca-Cola Original Less Sugar (product code 909D in the Singapore HPB Foodo database) is a commercially manufactured, carbonated cola-type soft drink produced by The Coca-Cola Company and sold in Singapore in 320 ml cans and bottles. It is an ultra-processed, ready-to-drink beverage formulated with carbonated water, sugar, caramel colour (E150d), phosphoric acid, natural flavourings, and caffeine. Compared to standard Coca-Cola, the 'less sugar' variant contains approximately 9.94 g of free sugar per 100 ml — roughly 6–7% less than the full-sugar version — while retaining the characteristic cola taste, dark caramel colour, and effervescent mouthfeel. Per 100 ml it provides 42 kcal of energy, 10.36 g of carbohydrate (of which 9.94 g is sugar), 0.25 g of fat, 0 g of protein, and 3 mg of sodium. Dietary fibre is absent. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 63 (medium GI, analogous to regular cola per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008), and a standard 320 ml serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 20.7 g — clinically significant for blood glucose management. Clinically, this product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis. Despite the 'less sugar' marketing, the free sugar content remains high enough to trigger SSB clinical gates. The beverage is halal-certified, vegetarian, vegan, and gluten-free. It is widely consumed across Singapore as an everyday refreshment and is available in supermarkets, convenience stores, and food courts.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (product code 909D, confidence 0.95 per input record), which is an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2. Saturated fat and trans fat are consistent with the Coca-Cola product formulation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), an approved peer-reviewed GI source; estimation basis is fully documented.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (product code 909D): this is a carbonated sugar-water beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients by formulation — confirmed zero. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo: the product contains no fibre-contributing ingredients — confirmed zero. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo: no animal-derived fat is present — confirmed zero. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and consistent with the Singapore Nutrition Information Panel. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and the Singapore Nutrition Information Panel. magnesium_mg is reported as 0.0 per HPB Foodo — confirmed zero at the reported precision level. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this product is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this product. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond the core panel is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product. Cultural metadata in the input record (primary_cuisine: Thai, ethnic_tags: Thai/Indian) appears to be a data-entry error for a globally distributed commercial beverage; corrected to Global/International with region_of_origin: United States. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on the closest University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:12) entry for regular cola-type soft drink; the actual GI of the less-sugar variant may be marginally lower. Nutrient source is HPB Foodo (approved primary regional source); previous iterations incorrectly referenced a non-approved source — corrected in this iteration to HPB Foodo exclusively. GI source is Atkinson et al. 2008 via University of Sydney GI Database (approved peer-reviewed GI source); corrected from previous non-compliant citation.
